explicit_text: I was born in Pasadena almost sixty years ago and have lived there off and on most of my life. I attended several schools in Pasadena, including Luther Burbank Grammar School and University School for high school. Even though I moved to Altadena for about twenty years, Pasadena has always been home to me and where I still live today.
implicit_text: I was born in a city near Los Angeles almost sixty years ago and have lived there off and on most of my life. I attended several schools in that city, including Luther Burbank Grammar School and University School for high school. Even though I moved to a neighboring community for about twenty years, that city has always been home to me and where I still live today.

explicit_text: After living in Pasadena, my family moved to Altadena where we stayed for about twenty years. I went to Charles W. Elliot Junior High School there. Altadena was a significant part of my youth and schooling before I returned to Pasadena for high school.
implicit_text: After living in the city near Los Angeles, my family moved to a nearby unincorporated area where we stayed for about twenty years. I went to Charles W. Elliot Junior High School there. That community was a significant part of my youth and schooling before I returned to the nearby city for high school.

explicit_text: I attended Woodbury College, now known as Woodbury University, right around the end of World War II. It was located on Wilshire Boulevard near Figueroa Street at the time and had expanded to accommodate many GI students. I studied commercial art there, though the program wasn't very strong, but it was a good place for me as a young and inexperienced student.
implicit_text: I attended a small college known for its commercial art program around the end of World War II. It was located on a major boulevard near a well-known street in downtown Los Angeles at the time and had expanded to accommodate many GI students. I studied commercial art there, though the program wasn't very strong, but it was a good place for me as a young and inexperienced student.

explicit_text: Woodbury College, where I studied commercial art, was located on Wilshire Boulevard near Figueroa Street during the late 1940s. This area was significant because it housed the expanded facilities to accommodate returning GI students after the war. I remember it as the place where I began my formal art education.

explicit_text: Figueroa Street was near the location of Woodbury College when I attended in the late 1940s. The college was on Wilshire Boulevard close to Figueroa, and this area was part of my early art education experience. It was a notable landmark for me during that time.
implicit_text: A well-known street in Los Angeles was near the location of the college I attended in the late 1940s. The school was on a major boulevard close to this street, and this area was part of my early art education experience. It was a notable landmark for me during that time.

explicit_text: After Woodbury, I went to ArtCenter College of Design for a semester. Back then, it was located way out on Third Street in what used to be an old girls' school. Unfortunately, I was drafted during that time, so my studies there were cut short.
implicit_text: After my first college, I went to a design school for a semester. Back then, it was located far out on Third Street in what used to be an old girls' school. Unfortunately, I was drafted during that time, so my studies there were cut short.

explicit_text: After serving in the Korean War, I attended Chouinard Art Institute, which I consider the best art school I went to. I was there during its peak, with Mrs. Chouinard and many prime teachers still active. It was a great experience, and looking back, I realize how fortunate I was to study there.
implicit_text: After serving in the Korean War, I attended a prestigious art institute in Los Angeles, which I consider the best art school I went to. I was there during its peak, with the founder and many prime teachers still active. It was a great experience, and looking back, I realize how fortunate I was to study there.

explicit_text: Don Graham was my most influential teacher at Chouinard Art Institute. He taught drawing and composition from a unique perspective, pushing students to tackle difficult art concepts rather than just copying. He also worked part-time at Disney in the early 1930s, hired by Walt Disney to improve the drawing skills of animators for Snow White, which shows his deep connection to both traditional art and animation.
implicit_text: One of my most influential teachers at that Los Angeles art institute taught drawing and composition from a unique perspective, pushing students to tackle difficult art concepts rather than just copying. He also worked part-time at a major animation studio in the early 1930s, hired by the studio's founder to improve the drawing skills of animators for their first feature film, which shows his deep connection to both traditional art and animation.

explicit_text: William Moore was another remarkable teacher I had at Chouinard who taught color and design. He was probably an interior designer by profession and had a unique system influenced by cubism, although he never used that term. His method involved painting colors on paper, cutting them out, and arranging them into designs, which was very instructional and helped me with book layout skills.

explicit_text: Walt Disney played a key role in bringing Don Graham to teach his animators in the early 1930s because he felt his artists lacked proper drawing skills for Snow White. Disney recognized the need for better-trained draftsmen to achieve his vision. Later, when I joined Disney in 1955, Don Graham was still connected to the studio, working part-time on his book The Art of Animation.
implicit_text: The founder of a major animation studio played a key role in bringing my drawing teacher to instruct his animators in the early 1930s because he felt his artists lacked proper drawing skills for their first feature film. He recognized the need for better-trained draftsmen to achieve his vision. Later, when I joined that studio in 1955, my teacher was still connected to it, working part-time on his book about animation art.

entity: Snow White and the Seven Dwarfs
explicit_text: Snow White and the Seven Dwarfs was a pivotal project for Disney in the early 1930s that required highly skilled artists. Walt Disney hired Don Graham to train his animators because their drawing skills were not strong enough for such a demanding feature. This film marked a turning point in animation, blending traditional art techniques with cartoon animation.
implicit_text: A groundbreaking animated feature film from the early 1930s was a pivotal project for a major studio that required highly skilled artists. The studio's founder hired my drawing teacher to train his animators because their drawing skills were not strong enough for such a demanding feature. This film marked a turning point in animation, blending traditional art techniques with cartoon animation.

explicit_text: Don Graham wrote The Art of Animation while he was working part-time at Disney in the mid to late 1950s. When I arrived at Disney in 1955, he was still there, working on the book in a studio room. The book was published around 1957 or 1958 and reflected his deep knowledge of art and animation.
implicit_text: My drawing teacher wrote a book about animation art while working part-time at the major animation studio in the mid to late 1950s. When I arrived at the studio in 1955, he was still there, working on the book in a studio room. The book was published around 1957 or 1958 and reflected his deep knowledge of art and animation.

explicit_text: Don Graham, my teacher at Chouinard and later a Disney instructor, was deeply steeped in the traditions of the Renaissance masters. He loved Renaissance art and brought that influence into his teaching, even when working with animators at Disney. It was quite a contrast for him to move from Renaissance art to the world of cartoons, but he learned a lot from the animators as well.

explicit_text: I was drafted during the Korean War, which interrupted my studies at ArtCenter College of Design. After my service, I returned to school and attended Chouinard Art Institute. The war was a significant event that shaped the timing of my education and career path.
